behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this behavior depends on real recursive watch behavior at the OS/filesystem event boundary, not just path-filter code. Rationale B (mock-hide): mocked watcher events can always claim subdirectory coverage and miss real inotify/FSEvents/non-recursive watcher failures. Rejection-gate: Integration was rejected because behavior text explicitly requires real placement in a subdirectory of the watched root, which is an OS watch-scope condition.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary watched directory tree, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this is path+time-window state logic inside dedup, a single-object/function concern. Rationale B (mock-hide): mocks do not hide the core bug here; deterministic clock-controlled unit tests can expose off-by-one/window-expiry defects directly. Rejection-gate: higher levels were rejected because behavior text is specifically about deduplication logic for same path within a window, with no required OS/network/subprocess condition.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate dedup component with injected clock, submit same path twice within window and once after expiry, and assert accept/reject decisions match configuration boundaries.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): the failure appears at real OS event coalescing/splitting boundaries. Rationale B (mock-hide): a fake watcher emitting one canonical event can hide duplicate-event storms that occur in real filesystems. Rejection-gate: Integration was rejected because behavior text explicitly says OS emits multiple filesystem events for one write.
behaviors.test_description: With a real watched directory, perform write patterns known to trigger multiple events (create+modify+close), capture daemon processing count for that file, and assert one pipeline execution.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this is watch-handle lifecycle under real directory deletion/recreation, an OS primitive boundary. Rationale B (mock-hide): mocked filesystem adapters can skip inode/watch invalidation behavior and falsely pass reattachment logic. Rejection-gate: Integration was rejected because behavior text explicitly requires remove-then-recreate while running, i.e., real runtime OS watcher recovery.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, delete it, recreate same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed after recreation without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this is ordering/state handoff across watcher intake, metadata lookup, and queue scheduler modules. Rationale B (mock-hide): a pure unit test of sort alone can miss pipeline handoff/order propagation bugs, but full OS-level behavior is not required if timestamps are controlled via test fixtures/stubs. Integration-brake check: (a) no mandatory real OS/network/subprocess requirement in text, (b) no AI/ML output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic chain. Rejection-gate: System was considered but rejected because behavior text constrains ordering policy (creation timestamp), not OS-specific watcher semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake+metadata+queue modules together with controlled file metadata (or temp files with settable fixture metadata), inject simultaneous arrivals, and assert processing sequence matches creation timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): correctness depends on real timing between filesystem events and ongoing writes. Rationale B (mock-hide): mocked write completion/state checks usually appear atomic and hide partial-write races, close/flush timing, and file-lock semantics. Rejection-gate: Integration was rejected because behavior text explicitly requires handling arrival during active write, a real OS I/O race condition.
behaviors.test_description: In a real watched directory, write a large file in chunks with delays so arrival event occurs mid-write; assert daemon defers processing until final chunk/close and output reflects complete content.
